title: How to Write AI Video Scripts and Prompts That Work
excerpt: Learn how to write better AI video scripts and prompts with story structure, visual language, shot planning, examples, and common mistakes to avoid.
metaTitle: How to Write AI Video Scripts and Prompts
metaDescription: Write better AI video scripts and prompts with clear structure, visual details, shot planning, examples, and consistency tips.
keywords: how to write ai video scripts

faqSchema.question: What makes a good AI video script?
faqSchema.answer: A good AI video script has a clear hook, visible actions, simple scene beats, and an ending. It should describe what the viewer sees, not only what the narrator thinks.

faqSchema.question: What should an AI video prompt include?
faqSchema.answer: Include subject, action, setting, camera direction, mood, style, lighting, and any consistency rules such as character reference or product details.

faqSchema.question: Should I write the script or prompt first?
faqSchema.answer: Write the script first if you are making a story or explainer. Write a prompt first only when you need a single visual clip without a broader sequence.

faqSchema.question: How do I keep AI video characters consistent?
faqSchema.answer: Use reference images, consistent character descriptions, stable style choices, and a storyboard workflow that keeps character details attached to each shot.

htmlContent: <article>
<section id='intro-section'>
Better scripts and prompts create better AI video

If your AI videos feel random, the problem may not be the model. It may be the input. Learning how to write AI video scripts and prompts gives the system clearer direction: what happens, what the viewer sees, how the camera moves, and what should stay consistent across shots.

A script explains the story. A prompt explains the image or clip. Strong AI video workflows connect the two. VidFab's Story-to-Video Workflow starts with the script, uses Gemini 3 Flash to break it into shots, then turns each shot into storyboard frames and video prompts.
This guide shows how to write scripts and prompts that are clear enough for AI to execute and structured enough for viewers to understand.
</section>
<section id='script-basics-section'>
What makes a good AI video script
A good AI video script is short, visual, and structured. It does not need cinematic language. It needs visible beats.
<ul>
• Hook: The first line creates curiosity or shows the problem.
• Setup: The viewer understands the subject and context.
• Change: Something moves, transforms, improves, breaks, or is revealed.
• Payoff: The video ends with a clear result or next step.

For example, instead of writing, small brands need better content, write: A small bakery owner watches three product photos fail online. Then a 15-second AI video turns the same cupcake into a warm, slow-motion product story.
</section>
<section id='story-first-section'>
Story-first vs asset-first planning
Story-first

Use story-first planning when the message matters. This works for explainers, faceless channels, tutorials, product stories, and YouTube Shorts. Write the script first, then generate the visuals that support it.
Asset-first

Use asset-first planning when you already have a product photo, character image, or brand visual. In that case, the prompt should describe how the existing asset moves, where it appears, and what should remain unchanged.
VidFab can support both approaches, but the story-to-video workflow is strongest when you want a sequence with a beginning, middle, and end.
</section>
<section id='prompt-structure-section'>
Prompt basics: structure and key elements
A useful AI video prompt usually includes:
<ul>
• Subject: Who or what is in the shot?
• Action: What is happening?
• Setting: Where does the shot take place?
• Camera: close-up, wide shot, tracking shot, overhead, slow push-in.
• Style: realistic, cinematic, anime, product commercial, documentary.
• Lighting: soft studio light, neon, golden hour, moody contrast.
• Continuity: character, product, logo, colors, or outfit that must stay consistent.
Do not overload every prompt with every detail. Include the details that affect the shot. If nothing should change about the character, make that clear. If the camera matters, say it directly.
</section>
<section id='examples-section'>
AI video script and prompt examples by use case
Product video
Script beat: A founder places a plain skincare bottle on a bathroom counter. In seconds, the scene transforms into a clean product demo with steam, soft light, and a clear before-and-after result.

Prompt: Close-up of a minimal skincare bottle on a marble bathroom counter, soft morning light, gentle steam, premium product commercial style, slow camera push-in, clean background, label remains sharp.
Social short
Script beat: Three bad hooks appear on screen and vanish. A stronger hook appears as the background shifts into a high-energy vertical video sequence.
Prompt: Vertical 9:16 social video, creator desk setup, floating text cards disappear one by one, energetic motion, bright contrast, fast pacing, modern creator studio.
Story video
Script beat: A tired student opens an old notebook and discovers the notes changing into animated scenes from the lesson.

Prompt: Cinematic close-up of a student's hands opening a worn notebook at night, handwritten notes glow and transform into tiny animated scenes above the page, warm desk lamp, magical realism, slow camera tilt.
</section>
<section id='mistakes-section'>
Common script and prompt mistakes
Writing concepts instead of visuals
Words like success, growth, or creativity are not visuals. Show what success looks like in the scene.
Changing style every shot
If each prompt uses a different style, the video feels stitched together. Keep style, lighting, and character details stable unless the change is intentional.
Adding too many actions
One shot should not contain five major actions. Split complex events into separate shots.
Forgetting the platform
A prompt for vertical TikTok should frame subjects differently from a landscape YouTube explainer.
</section>
<section id='consistency-section'>
How to improve consistency over multiple videos
<ul>
• Create a reusable character description.
• Use reference images when available.
• Keep style and lighting terms consistent.
• Use a storyboard workflow so each shot inherits the same creative direction.
• Save prompts that worked and reuse their structure.
Consistency is less about writing a longer prompt and more about keeping the same visual rules across every generation.
<div class='cta-box'>
Turn your script into planned video shots
VidFab analyzes your script, creates storyboard frames, and keeps each shot tied to the story.
Write and Generate ->
</section>
<section id='faq-section'>
FAQ
How long should an AI video script be?
For a first short video, write 60 to 90 words. Keep it focused on one idea and one visual sequence.
Should prompts be detailed or simple?
Prompts should be specific, not bloated. Include subject, action, setting, camera, style, and continuity details that matter for the shot.
Can AI write video prompts for me?
Yes, but review them. AI-generated prompts are better when the script already has strong visual beats.
What is the easiest way to improve AI video output?
Rewrite the script in more visual terms and review storyboard frames before final video generation.
</section>
</article>
